It emphasizes that the material is protected by copyright and other intellectual property rights, requiring a license for commercial exploitation. Reproduction or use without modification is permitted solely for informational purposes, while other uses necessitate written permission. The work is explicitly intended for automotive applications and has not been developed or tested for non-automotive uses. Additionally, "AUTOSAR" and its logo are registered trademarks.

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary: In 2022, ASAM released the first version of the Diagnostic SOVD Standard, which outlines how AUTOSAR realizes SOVD and provides use-case-centric guidance beyond the Functional Cluster ara::diag to address its holistic nature. SOVD's realization is feasible only on HPC machines due to technologies like HTTPS, yet its protocol is defined at the vehicle level, raising challenges for integrating microcontrollers typically diagnosed with UDS. Chapter

nodes.title: Brief summary of ASAM SOVD
nodes.summary: The section provides a brief overview of ASAM SOVD, highlighting its introduction as a new standard to address specific industry needs. It outlines the motivation for developing SO

nodes.title: Motivation
nodes.summary: The automotive industry's current diagnostic protocol, UDS (ISO 14229), prioritizes efficiency by offloading data interpretation to client-side ODX files, creating challenges for client technology stacks and strong dependencies on external ODX data. SOVD addresses these

structure.title: SOVD Reference Architecture
structure.summary: The SOVD Reference Architecture is designed to support a central SOVD edge node in a distributed system by requiring specific infrastructural components. It organizes functions into clustered blocks, as illustrated in Figure 2.1. The architecture comprises three main components: the SOVD Gateway, which serves as the entry point; the Diagnostic Manager, responsible for diagnostic functionalities; and the SOVD to UDS Translation, which handles protocol conversion between SOVD and UDS. These components work together to enable the system's operational capabilities.

nodes.title: SOVD Gateway
nodes.summary: The SOVD Gateway serves as an edge node, routing client requests to internal SOVD endpoints based on the entity part of the URI. It extracts this entity to determine the target endpoint, which can be statically configured or dynamically discovered via mDNS. Forwarding occurs at the application layer as an HTTP reverse proxy. Configuration is managed through the SOVDGatewayInstantiation in the TPS_Manifest, which defines external client connections and internal forwarding targets. The reference architecture (Figure 2.1) illustrates this setup.

nodes.title: Diagnostic Manager
nodes.summary: The Diagnostic Manager originally managed diagnostic services and fault memory per ISO 14229-1 but now also natively supports SOVD as an SOVD server. Its design reuses existing UDS functionality while enabling native SOVD support, with multiple Diagnostic Server Instances allowing independent Software Clusters. Each DEXT Diagnostic Contribution Set corresponds to a server instance with a unique Diagnostic Address, a principle adopted by SOVD. The Diagnostic Manager acts as an SOVD component, routing requests to SQVD subcomponents, with configuration defined

nodes.title: SOVD to UDS Translation
nodes.summary: The functional block translates SOVD commands to UDS requests using predefined ODX definitions for SOVD-to-UDS mapping. It operates as an on-board test client, sending UDS requests to the target diagnostic address and forwarding translated UDS responses to SOVD clients. The translation details are standardized by ASAM, which defines the complexity of implementation. AUTOSAR references the ASAM standard without specifying implementation details for this block.

nodes.title: Backend Connectivity
nodes.summary: SOVD supports proximity, remote, and in-vehicle diagnostics, with proximity and in-vehicle access standardized via mDNS. Remote access standardization is complex due to backend dependencies, though AUTOSAR maintains flexibility. A backend connectivity functional block abstracts backend connections, routing SOVD requests to the SOVD Gateway. Discovery of the Gateway is achieved through mDNS, while routing is implemented via HTTP forwarding.

structure.title: SOVD use cases
structure.summary: The section titled "SOVD use cases" outlines how these use cases are designed and implemented at the level of a Diagnostic Server Instance. It focuses on the mechanisms and processes through which SOVD functionality is achieved within this specific system context. The text emphasizes the structural and operational aspects of SOVD use cases as they relate to diagnostic server instances. No additional technical details beyond the described scope are included. The chapter provides an overview of the design principles and realization of SOVD use cases within the diagnostic server framework.

nodes.title: Common use cases in SOVD and UDS

structure.title: A big portion of the SOVD use cases can be mapped in a straightforward fashion to existing UDS use cases. How this mapping has been done in the context of the Diagnostic manager is displayed in SWS Diagnostics. For cases matching UDS (ISO 14229-1) one of the main principles was introduced: Reuse of the same port instance as used for the corresponding UDS service. This is especially convenient for designing applications since no further ports need to be integrated with redundant functionality. Also, for these kinds of implementations the same rules regarding reentrancy and parallel execution as for UDS apply. SOVD handling of some use-cases may be sufficiently different from their UDS counterparts requiring differentiated requirements and mechanisms. Also, in terms of methodology these SOVD methods are adopting the existing configuration mechanism by using DEXT and only extending the DEXT at certain needed places. This matching has been done in the following way:
structure.summary: The SOVD use cases are mapped to UDS (ISO 14229-1) by reusing the same port instance as for corresponding UDS services, simplifying application design and adhering to UDS reentrancy and

nodes.title: SOVD-specific use cases
nodes.summary: The Diagnostic Manager supports SOVD-specific use cases not covered by ISO 14229-1, introducing two new interfaces for Authorization and Proximity Challenge. SOVD use case data-lists are natively handled by the Diagnostic Manager. Additional SOVD-specific use cases will be integrated in future releases. These interfaces and native handling ensure compliance with SOVD requirements while extending diagnostic capabilities beyond ISO 14229-1. No other interfaces or functionalities are mentioned for these use cases.

nodes.title: Access Permissions
nodes.summary: Authorization in SOVD relies on OAuth tokens within request headers, which the Diagnostic Manager uses via the `SovdAuthorization` interface to retrieve an encoded authorization role from the application. This role determines if the SOVD Client is permitted to execute the requested SOVD. The DEXT model for ISO 14229-1 Service 0x29 defines roles like `DiagnosticAuthRoles` and maps `DiagnosticAccessPermission` to specific SOVD methods. This mapping is established based on existing services in the Diagnostic Manager’s SOVD section, ensuring accurate associations between permissions and method execution. The relationship between roles and SOVD methods is critical for enforcing access control.

nodes.title: Proximity Challenge
nodes.summary: The SOVD proximity challenge is a mechanism used by the SOVD server to verify a client's proximity before executing a SOVD operation. The SovdProximityChallenge interface delegates the challenge procedure to the application and notifies the Diagnostic Manager of the result. The Diagnostic Manager then proceeds with the operation based on the challenge outcome. Configuration for requiring a proximity challenge is managed by DEXT via a special data group (SDG), with detailed specifications referenced in SWS_Diagnostics.

nodes.title: Software Update
nodes.summary: The Software and Configuration Update functionality is implemented via the AUTOSAR UCM framework, supporting two update types: **vehicle updates** (coordinated via UCM campaign using VUCM and Vehicle Package) and **component updates** (individual via UCM Diagnostic Application on Adaptive Machines). Vehicle updates route requests through the SOVD Gateway to an OTA client, which autonomously manages the update process using backend-provided packages, with the SOVD server controlling the flow. Component updates rely on the Diagnostic Manager’s `ara::diag` interface, mapped to `ara::ucm::PackageManagement`, enabling request forwarding and service registration. Unlike vehicle updates, component updates require active SOVD Client involvement across stages like package transfer, processing, activation, and resource deletion. Implementation specifics for OTA clients are vendor-specific and

nodes.title: Logging
nodes.summary: The logging functionality in the system is not standardized by ara::log, leading to vendor-specific implementations for logging file retrieval and management. The connection between ara::diag and ara::log, particularly for reading logging files, adheres to platform vendor guidelines and requirements. These vendor-specific approaches dictate how logging files are handled, ensuring compliance with proprietary specifications. The text references Figure 3.1, which outlines the solution approach for SOVD updates, though it does not elaborate on the logging details beyond vendor-specific implementation.

nodes.title: Bulk Data
nodes.summary: The Diagnostic Manager, via the ara::diag interface, oversees bulk data operations. The Adaptive application supports these operations by integrating persistence mechanisms and cryptographic functions like signature generation and hash verification. It enables secure, controlled storage, retrieval, and deletion of bulk data. The cryptographic functionalities ensure data integrity and security during these processes. The system relies on the Adaptive application's capabilities to manage data lifecycle operations effectively.

nodes.title: Configuration
nodes.summary: The ASAM configuration handling involves two methods: parameter-based and bulk-data approaches. Bulk-data support is implemented via the `ara::diag::GenericConfiguration` interface, which fully incorporates the ASAM SOVD standard's methods. This interface enables the Diagnostic Manager to execute operations (e.g., persistence, cryptographic actions) based on access control permissions. Adaptive applications leverage this interface for streamlined configuration management, ensuring compliance with ASAM SOVD while offering flexibility in automotive systems. The interface simplifies bulk-data operations while maintaining standard conformity and security.
